rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is explicit_self, so the row is climate-relevant even if the primary gate missed an outcome token. The measures are clearly resource-efficiency / waste-prevention actions: the verbatim text commits to 'generalising the use of washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches' (replacing disposable nappies with reusable ones) and 'replacement of plastic crockery with durable materials' (durable/reusable alternatives to plastic items). These directly align with the stated mechanism waste_prevention_and_reuse_program → waste_generation_reduction. Therefore the deterministic failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token found') is a false negative and the row should be kept.
policy_statement: Eco-crèches: generalise washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches; continue replacing plastic crockery with durable materials in 100% of eco-crèches; and use eco-responsible care/cleaning approaches across 100% of eco-crèches.
mechanism_description: The city expands reusable nappies, replaces plastic crockery with durable materials, and uses eco-responsible care/cleaning practices in eco-crèches to reduce single-use waste and the emissions associated with producing and disposing of those materials.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (childcare/food service) → ↓ material throughput (single-use products) → ↓ embodied energy & upstream GHG emissions (and ↓ waste-treatment emissions)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Childcare facilities and city programs substitute reusable nappies and durable tableware for disposable items and adopt lower-waste procurement/cleaning routines.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for virgin material extraction, manufacturing, and transportation of disposable goods; reduced waste generation lowers collection/processing needs and landfill/incineration loads.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions, dominated by avoided production (embodied carbon) and supplemented by avoided disposal emissions and operational energy in waste management.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided upstream GHG emissions from manufacturing and supplying fewer single-use products due to waste prevention and reuse.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Deliver the same childcare and meal service with fewer single-use materials, cutting embodied energy and lifecycle GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While mitigation occurs, the mechanism acts mainly by reducing resource and material throughput (a classic resource-efficiency lever) rather than directly controlling an emission source (e.g., landfill methane capture) or managing climate hazards (adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (NBS).  
3) Edge cases applied: If reuse systems significantly increase laundering energy/water, the net benefit depends on implementation (energy source, load efficiency), but the intended and typical dominant effect remains reduced lifecycle emissions via waste prevention.  
4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy framed as “waste prevention and reuse → reduced waste generation” shares the same primary resource-efficiency pathway of avoided material production and disposal.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy’s secondary classification leans more toward upstream manufacturing avoidance vs. downstream disposal avoidance requires policy-level evidence (materials displaced, laundering/cleaning energy, waste treatment baseline).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/offset if reusables require energy- or water-intensive laundering using high-carbon energy, or if durable goods are over-specified (high embodied carbon) and not used enough cycles; classification remains Resource Efficiency because the mechanism’s core is reducing material throughput for the same service.
key_indicators: “generalising the use of washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches”; “Extend the use of washable nappies to 100% of eco-crèches”; “replacement of plastic crockery with durable materials in 100% of eco-crèches”; “use eco-responsible products/diaper-changing techniques in 100% of the city’s eco-crèches”
co_benefits: Reduced solid waste and litter; potential reductions in plastic pollution; improved indoor environmental quality from “eco-responsible” cleaning products; possible cost savings over time from reusables.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a procurement requirement (durable crockery, eco-responsible products) or operational mandate for municipal facilities; classified as target-setting because the text is framed as quantified coverage objectives (“100%”) rather than explicit purchasing rules or legal obligations. Mention of “eco-responsible” cleaning is ambiguous (could include toxics reduction more than climate).
